{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Niiyama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Niiyama Shrine hall of worship 2016-11-11 01.jpg|250px|拝殿]]
Worship Hall |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga }} [[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]] '''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Kanzaki Town, {{ill|Kanzaki City|ja|神埼市|12=draft}}, Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}} was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}} was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|12=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐|12=draft}}, prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=draft}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友義鎮|12=draft}} but was later rebuilt with the support of {{ill|Nabeshima Naoshige|ja|鍋島直茂|12=draft}} and his son {{ill|Nabeshima Katsushige|ja|鍋島勝茂|12=draft}}. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from {{ill|Kanzaki Station|ja|神埼駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Nagasaki Main Line|ja|長崎本線|12=draft}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Niiyama Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Niiyama Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]]

The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from {{ill|Kanzaki Station|ja|神埼駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Nagasaki Main Line|ja|長崎本線|qq=draft}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Niiyama Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]]

Worship Hall |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga }} [[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]] '''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in Kanzaki Town, {{ill|Kanzaki City|ja|神埼市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki City|qq=}}, Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsunoo Taisha|qq=}} was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ennin|qq=}} (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang China|qq=}}, prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}} here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友義鎮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}} but was later rebuilt with the support of {{ill|Nabeshima Naoshige|ja|鍋島直茂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nabeshima Naoshige|qq=}} and his son {{ill|Nabeshima Katsushige|ja|鍋島勝茂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nabeshima Katsushige|qq=}}. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Niiyama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Niiyama Shrine hall of worship 2016-11-11 01.jpg|250px|拝殿]]
Worship Hall |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga }} [[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]] '''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]]. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:日吉神社]] [[Category:神埼市の建築物]]

The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:日吉神社]] [[Category:神埼市の建築物]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]]

Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]]. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]]

Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}[[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]]. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}[[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]]. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:佐賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}[[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]]. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]]. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}[[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]]. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:佐賀県内のMunicipal designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:神埼市の歴史]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kanzaki]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社). == History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}} During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]]. Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines. == Cultural Properties == === Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}} === Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}} * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty. === Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan. == Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine] {{translated page|ja|仁比山神社|version=102285963|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[sn:Niiyama Shrine]] [[ja:仁比山神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Sannō-jinja]] [[Category:Buildings in Kanzaki, Saga]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kanzaki]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] {{Commons category|Niiyama-jinja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Niiyama-jinja]][[ja:仁比山神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24875740]] Wikidata name: Niiyama Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767}} |name = Niiyama Shrine |image = File:Niiyama Shrine hall of worship 2016-11-11 01.jpg |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga |prefecture= {{ill|Kanzaki|qid=Q990392}} |coordinates= {{Coord|33.358336|N|130.364853|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
[[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]]
'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.

[[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]]
'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].

During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].

'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>

Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />

'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiyama-jinja]][[ja:仁比山神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24875740]] Wikidata name: Niiyama Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} |name = Niiyama Shrine |image = File:Niiyama Shrine hall of worship 2016-11-11 01.jpg |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga |prefecture= {{ill|Kanzaki|WD=Q990392}} |coordinates= {{Coord|33.358336|N|130.364853|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
[[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]]
'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine]

The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].

[[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]]
'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kanzaki Town, [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki City]], Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo Taisha]] was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].

In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang China]], prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of [[:en:Ōtomo Sōrin|Ōtomo Sōrin]] but was later rebuilt with the support of [[:en:Nabeshima Naoshige|Nabeshima Naoshige]] and his son [[:en:Nabeshima Katsushige|Nabeshima Katsushige]].<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from [[:en:Kanzaki Station (Saga)|Kanzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Nagasaki Main Line|Nagasaki Main Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine]

'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Kanzaki Town, {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki City}}, Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsunoo Taisha}} was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|en|Ōtomo Sōrin|WD=Q1185032|lt=Ōtomo Sōrin}} but was later rebuilt with the support of {{ill|Nabeshima Naoshige|en|Nabeshima Naoshige|WD=Q1039095|lt=Nabeshima Naoshige}} and his son {{ill|Nabeshima Katsushige|en|Nabeshima Katsushige|WD=Q3138980|lt=Nabeshima Katsushige}}.<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.

The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>

The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from {{ill|Kanzaki Station (Saga)|en|Kanzaki Station (Saga)|WD=Q5358654|lt=Kanzaki Station}} on the {{ill|Nagasaki Main Line|en|Nagasaki Main Line|WD=Q1078117|lt=Nagasaki Main Line}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiyama-jinja]][[ja:仁比山神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiyama-jinja]][[ja:仁比山神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiyama-jinja]][[ja:仁比山神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} |name = Niiyama Shrine |image = File:Niiyama Shrine hall of worship 2016-11-11 01.jpg |location = 1692 Teki, Kanzaki Town, Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture, Japan |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33|LatMin=21|LatSec=30.01 |Longitude = 130|LongMin=21|LongSec=53.47 |LabelPos = bottom |Deities = {{nihongo|Ōyamakui no Mikoto|大山咋命}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Traditionally dated to 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) |Alias = Sannō Hiyoshi Shrine, Sannō-san |Festivals = April 26 (festival), Shikinen Taisai (Da-ota Festival every 12 years) |Map = Japan Saga |prefecture= {{ill|Kanzaki|WD=Q990392}} |coordinates= {{Coord|33.358336|N|130.364853|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
[[File:Niomon of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Niō Gate]] [[File:Torii and camphor trees of Niiyama Shrine.jpg|thumb|200px|Second torii and camphor trees]]
'''Niiyama Shrine''' (仁比山神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Kanzaki Town, {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki City}}, Saga Prefecture, Japan. Its historical Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Prefectural Shrine (県社).
== History == According to tradition, Niiyama Shrine was established in 729 AD (Tenpyō 1) when the deity of {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsunoo Taisha}} was enshrined here. In 844 AD (Shōwa 11), {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) reportedly discovered an inscription of "Hiyoshi Shrine" upon his return from {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}, prompting Emperor Ninmyō to enshrine the deity of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} here as well. The name "Niiyama" is said to derive from the emperor's name (仁) combined with the mountain's association with Hie (比山).<ref name="角川1">{{Cite book|editor=角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会 |title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 41.佐賀県 |publisher=[[角川書店]] |year=1982 |isbn=978-4-04-622957-1}}</ref><ref name="市史1">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第1巻 (自然・民俗・石造物編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
Previously called "Hiyoshi Shrine" or "Sannō Shrine," it was closely associated with {{ill|Niiyama Gokokuji|ja|仁比山護国寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiyama Gokokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and functioned as a {{nihongo|jingūji|神宮寺|Shinto-Buddhist shrine-temple complex}}. The shrine was a site of devotion for local and imperial patrons and received land and offerings over the centuries, as documented in historical records.<ref name="市史2">{{Cite book|editor=神埼市教育委員会市史編纂室 |title=神埼市史 第2巻 (原始・古代、中世、近世編) |publisher=神埼市 |year=2022}}</ref>
During the {{nihongo|Eiroku era|永禄|1558–1570}}, the shrine was burned down by forces of {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|en|Ōtomo Sōrin|WD=Q1185032|lt=Ōtomo Sōrin}} but was later rebuilt with the support of {{ill|Nabeshima Naoshige|en|Nabeshima Naoshige|WD=Q1039095|lt=Nabeshima Naoshige}} and his son {{ill|Nabeshima Katsushige|en|Nabeshima Katsushige|WD=Q3138980|lt=Nabeshima Katsushige}}.<ref name="角川1" />
Following the {{nihongo|Shinto-Buddhist Separation Order|神仏分離令}}, the shrine was renamed Niiyama Shrine in 1911 after absorbing other local shrines.<ref name="市史1" />
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * '''Ōta-mai (御田舞)''': A ritual dance performed during the {{nihongo|Da-ota Festival|大御田祭}}, held every 12 years in the {{nihongo|Year of the Monkey|申年}}. The dance involves 48 participants performing agricultural-themed movements to pray for a bountiful harvest.<ref name="県1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html |title=県指定（民俗文化財の部）02 |publisher=Saga Prefecture Cultural Database}}</ref>
=== Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Niō Gate (仁王門)''': Built in the {{nihongo|Kanbun era|寛文|1661–1673}}, the gate features two wooden {{nihongo|Niō guardians|仁王像}} over 3 meters tall. The gate was relocated during road construction in the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=1032 |title=Niō Gate of Niiyama Shrine |publisher=Kanzaki City Digital Museum}}</ref> * '''Camphor Trees''': Designated as a city natural monument, these ancient trees are integral to the shrine's natural beauty.<ref name="市史1" />
=== Nearby Landmarks === * '''Niiyama Gokokuji''': A historic temple adjacent to the shrine. * '''Kyūnen-an''': A scenic garden west of the shrine. * '''Ito Genboku's Residence''': The birthplace of the 19th-century physician and pioneer of Western medicine in Japan.
== Access == The shrine is a 15-minute drive from {{ill|Kanzaki Station (Saga)|en|Kanzaki Station (Saga)|WD=Q5358654|lt=Kanzaki Station}} on the {{ill|Nagasaki Main Line|en|Nagasaki Main Line|WD=Q1078117|lt=Nagasaki Main Line}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.kanzaki-museum.com/frmDataDetail.aspx?id=9313 Kanzaki City Digital Museum - Niiyama Shrine]
